summary:
The Dispilio Tablet is a wooden artifact discovered in 1993 at the submerged Neolithic lake settlement of Dispilio, near Kastoria in northern Greece, and dated by radiocarbon analysis to approximately 5260 BC. The tablet bears a series of incised marks, lines, and symbols whose function remains debated among scholars. Some researchers have proposed that the markings represent an early form of writing or proto-writing, while others interpret them as decorative or tally marks without linguistic content.

The site of Dispilio itself is a pile-dwelling settlement — one of many such lakeside communities that existed across Greece and the Balkans during the Neolithic period. Preserved underwater conditions at Dispilio have allowed the survival of organic materials, including wooden objects, that would otherwise have decayed, giving archaeologists an unusually detailed picture of everyday life. Whether or not the tablet's markings constitute true writing, the artifact is significant as evidence of symbolic notation in prehistoric Greece and contributes to ongoing debates about the origins and diffusion of writing in the ancient world.
